questions.question: The Second Five Year Plan (1956-61) was based on the model by:
options: P.C. Mahalanobis — emphasized heavy industrialization (steel plants: Bhilai, Durgapur, Rourkela) and public sector growth
questions.explanation: Second FYP (Mahalanobis Plan, 1956-61): focused on heavy industry and capital goods (Feldman-Mahalanobis model — investment in capital goods to boost long-term growth). Steel plants: Bhilai (Soviet assistance), Durgapur (British), Rourkela (West German). Also established IITs and other institutions.

questions.question: The Make in India initiative was launched by:
options: PM Narendra Modi in September 2014 — to make India a global manufacturing hub; focuses on 25 sectors; lion (Ashoka Chakra wheel as wheels) is its symbol
questions.explanation: Make in India (September 25, 2014): India as global design and manufacturing hub. 25 focus sectors: automobiles, aviation, chemicals, IT, pharma, construction, food processing, textiles, etc. The iconic logo: walking lion made of cogwheels (industry symbol). Complemented by: Invest India, Startup India, Skill India, Digital India.

questions.question: The Union Budget is prepared by which Ministry?
options: Ministry of Finance — specifically the Department of Economic Affairs (DEA) and Department of Revenue coordinate the budget; Finance Minister presents to Parliament
questions.explanation: Union Budget preparation: Ministry of Finance (DEA — Budget Division) issues budget circular to all ministries for expenditure estimates; Department of Revenue handles tax proposals (Direct Tax: CBDT, Indirect Tax: CBIC). Finance Minister consults stakeholders, states, industry. Finance Bill (tax changes) + Appropriation Bill (expenditure) presented together.

questions.question: The concept of 'infant industry protection' argues:
options: New/young industries in developing countries need temporary tariff protection to grow and become internationally competitive — used by USA (19th century), Germany (Bismarck era), Japan, South Korea, and now by developing economies including India
questions.explanation: Infant Industry Argument (Friedrich List, 1841 — 'National System of Political Economy'): nascent industries with potential scale economies need temporary protection (tariffs, subsidies) against established foreign competitors until they achieve global competitiveness. USA protected its manufacturing (1800s-1900s) with high tariffs. South Korea's POSCO steel. Counter: protection tends to become permanent, breeds inefficiency.

questions.question: The 'marginal cost of funds based lending rate' (MCLR) is different from 'base rate' in that MCLR:
options: Uses marginal cost of funds (current deposit rates at margin, not average cost) to set lending rate — more dynamic, passes monetary policy changes faster to borrowers than base rate (which used average cost)
Is the same as base rate
Is set by government not banks
questions.explanation: MCLR (April 2016): replaces Base Rate (April 2010). Base Rate used average cost of funds (lagged, slow to change). MCLR uses: marginal cost of deposits (current rates, not historical average), CRR maintenance cost, operating cost, and tenor premium. Changed monthly (MCLR) vs quarterly (Base Rate). Since EBLR (2019): retail and MSME loans linked to external benchmark (repo rate) — fastest transmission.

questions.question: The National Credit Guarantee Trust (NCGTC) oversees:
options: Credit guarantees for MSME loans (without collateral) — provides guarantee cover to lending institutions so they lend to MSMEs; also operates PM Mudra Yojana and PM SVANidhi guarantees
questions.explanation: NCGTC (National Credit Guarantee Trustee Company, 2014): provides credit guarantees for MSME loans through: Credit Guarantee Fund Trust for Micro and Small Enterprises (CGTMSE), Credit Guarantee Fund for Skill Development (CGFSD), Credit Guarantee Fund for Educational Loans (CGFEL), Credit Guarantee Scheme for Start-Ups. Borrowers don't need collateral; banks get guaranteed coverage.

questions.question: Structural unemployment is caused by:
options: Mismatch between workers' skills and available jobs due to technological change
questions.explanation: Structural unemployment: skills mismatch due to technology changes, industry shifts, or globalization. Long-term; requires retraining. E.g., manufacturing workers displaced by automation.

questions.question: The Bombay Plan (1944) was drafted by Indian industrialists including Tata and Birla, proposing:
options: Free market economy with no government intervention
Central planning with significant state investment in industry
questions.explanation: Bombay Plan: 8 industrialists (JRD Tata, GD Birla, etc.) proposed a 15-year plan with heavy state investment in infrastructure and industry, import substitution, and mixed economy framework.

questions.question: India's Wholesale Price Index (WPI) is published by:

questions.explanation: WPI: published monthly by Office of the Economic Adviser (OEA), Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT), Ministry of Commerce and Industry. Base year 2011-12, 697 commodities.

questions.question: The MSME sector in India is defined under the MSME Development (Amendment) Act 2020 based on:
options: Investment in plant & machinery/equipment AND annual turnover
questions.explanation: MSME 2020 definition: Micro (Investment ≤Rs 1 cr, Turnover ≤Rs 5 cr), Small (Investment ≤Rs 10 cr, Turnover ≤Rs 50 cr), Medium (Investment ≤Rs 50 cr, Turnover ≤Rs 250 cr). Composite criteria (investment + turnover).

questions.question: The Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT) is responsible for:
options: Formulating and implementing India's Foreign Trade Policy (FTP)
questions.explanation: DGFT: under Ministry of Commerce and Industry. Implements FTP, issues Importer Exporter Code (IEC), administers export promotion schemes (EPCG, Advance Authorization, MEIS/RODTEP). Also issues export/import licenses.

questions.question: The Index of Industrial Production (IIP) uses which base year and is published by:
questions.explanation: IIP: base year 2011-12 (revised from 2004-05 in 2017). Published monthly by NSO (National Statistical Office) under MoSPI. Measures growth in industry: Mining (14.4%), Manufacturing (77.6%), Electricity (8%).

questions.question: India's Industrial Policy Resolution of 1956 classified industries into schedules. Schedule A industries were exclusively reserved for:
questions.explanation: IPR 1956: Schedule A (17 industries) — exclusive State domain (arms, atomic energy, railways, coal, steel). Schedule B — State to establish, private allowed. Schedule C — private sector.

questions.explanation: P.C. Mahalanobis (1893-1972): statistician and planner, architect of India's second Five Year Plan (1956-61). Founder of ISI (Indian Statistical Institute). His 'two-sector' model emphasized heavy industry.
